How working within tight constraints often produces the most elegant and memorable solutions.

There's a paradox at the heart of creative work: **the more freedom you have, the harder it is to make something great**.

When everything is possible, nothing is chosen. The blank canvas is terrifying precisely because of its infinite potential. But give a designer a single color, or a poet a strict meter, and suddenly clarity emerges from constraint.

## The Grid as Liberation

I spent three years avoiding grid systems. They felt rigid, corporate, bureaucratic — antithetical to the kind of raw, alive work I wanted to make. Then I spent a week studying Swiss typography from the 1950s and everything changed.

The masters of Basel weren't imprisoned by their grids. They were *freed* by them. The grid handled the micro-decisions so the mind could focus on the macro ones. Where does the reader's eye go? What matters most? What can be stripped away?

## Constraints as Concept

The best constraints aren't arbitrary — they emerge from the problem itself.

A mobile interface constrained to a single thumb's reach isn't fighting the user; it's *respecting* them. A writing constraint that forces you to eliminate adjectives isn't punishing you; it's demanding that your nouns do real work.

When I design now, I start by asking: *what are the necessary constraints here?* Not the artificial ones I could impose, but the ones the project itself implies. The answer usually tells me everything about the direction.

## The Permission to Stop

Perhaps the deepest gift of constraints is that they tell you when you're done.

Without boundaries, work expands to fill all available time. With them, you reach completion — a genuine, recognizable endpoint where the work is finished, not abandoned.

That's the thing nobody tells you when you're starting out: done is not the enemy of perfect. Done *is* perfect, when the constraints were right to begin with.

Why sharing your half-formed ideas is braver and more useful than waiting until you're ready.

I published my first tutorial when I barely knew what I was talking about.

It was about CSS flexbox — a topic that now seems almost trivially simple — and I was wrong about three things in it. The comments corrected me within hours. It was mortifying and, also, the best learning experience I'd had in years.

## The Myth of Readiness

We're taught that expertise precedes expression. Learn first, share second. Master the craft, *then* show your work.

But this model misunderstands how knowledge actually forms. Understanding doesn't arrive complete and polished. It accretes, slowly, through application and feedback. The act of explaining something is part of how you understand it.

The teacher learns more than the student. Not because they're smarter, but because articulation forces precision.

## What You Lose by Waiting

Every idea has an expiration date — not in the sense that it stops being true, but in the sense that it stops being *yours to give*.

When you wait until you're certain, you wait until someone else has already said it better. The version of the idea that only you could have expressed, from exactly where you were standing, at exactly that moment — that version is gone.

## The Compound Effect of Small Shares

I have a note from a stranger who said a half-finished essay I wrote in 2022 helped them get through a difficult month. I barely remember writing it. It felt incomplete to me — I never finished the argument I was building toward.

They didn't need the finished argument. They needed exactly that particular halfway-there thing, that honest in-progress artifact of someone working something out.

That's what learning in public actually offers: not expertise, but *process*. And sometimes process is what people need most.

---

*Share the draft. Publish the question. Post the half-formed thing. The alternative is silence, and silence helps no one.*

The Compound Effect of Small Shares
Why I Rewrote My Site in Astro
A frank look at the tradeoffs of choosing a framework — and why static-first finally won me over.

My portfolio has been rebuilt seven times. React, Next.js, Gatsby, plain HTML, Svelte, Hugo, and now Astro. Each time, I told myself this was the last one.

I'm not naive enough to say that this time again. But I *am* willing to explain why Astro has made me happier than anything else.

## The Problem with JavaScript Frameworks for Content Sites

React is extraordinary. For highly interactive applications — dashboards, tools, anything with complex client state — it remains my first choice. But a portfolio site is not a complex interactive application.

It's text. Images. The occasional animation. A contact form.

Shipping 300KB of JavaScript to display my face and some words about what I do always felt like an architectural mismatch. Like driving a race car to the grocery store.


## What Astro Gets Right

Astro ships **zero JavaScript by default**. Components render to static HTML at build time. JavaScript is opt-in, only where interaction actually requires it.

This isn't a new idea — Hugo and Eleventy have done this for years. What Astro adds is the ability to use component frameworks (React, Svelte, Vue) in islands of interactivity when you need them, while keeping everything else static.

The mental model is clean. The output is fast. The developer experience is genuinely pleasant.

## The Numbers

Before Astro (Next.js): Lighthouse performance score of 71. First Contentful Paint: 2.3s.

After Astro: Lighthouse performance score of 99. First Contentful Paint: 0.4s.

Same content. Same design. Different architecture.

## The Tradeoffs

Astro isn't for everything. If your site is mostly application — authentication, real-time data, complex user state — Astro's model starts to fight you. The island architecture gets awkward when most of your page is an island.

But for portfolios, blogs, documentation, marketing sites? It's close to ideal.

I'll probably rebuild this site again someday. That's just who I am. But for now, I'm genuinely glad I chose Astro.
